sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient presents with a 3-month history of cough, which initially started after loading horses and worsened after a cold.
The patient reports that the cough has gone away on its own and has not returned.
The patient has a history of bronchitis and a lung nodule found incidentally on a chest X-ray.
The patient is a non-smoker, having quit 7 years ago, and has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
The patient reports no pain or shortness of breath.
Family history includes breast cancer in the patient's mother, but no lung cancer.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis, which is currently under control with methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
The patient was a previous smoker, having quit 7 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient denies any pain or shortness of breath.
Current medications: The patient takes methotrexate 7.5mg weekly.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 124/76, heart rate 70, respiration rate 16, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, afebrile.
Physical exam: The lungs are clear and equal bilateral.
The heart has a regular rate and rhythm, with no clicks, rubs, or murmurs.
The extremities have intact pulses, and capillary refill is brisk.
The patient has a little bit of swelling in the knuckles on both hands, which is consistent with rheumatoid arthritis.

Test Results: The patient had a chest CT scan that showed a solitary 2cm nodule in the lateral aspect of the right upper lobe, which appears smooth in appearance and has no evidence of emphysematous disease.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has an incidentally found right upper lobe lung nodule.
Problem list: Lung nodule; Rheumatoid arthritis; Previous smoking history.
Plan:
I ordered a pulmonary function test (PFT) to establish a baseline for lung function.
I also scheduled a PET CT to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.

I plan to remove the nodule via video-assisted thoracoscopy, which will involve making three small incisions on the side of the patient's right chest, and removing the nodule along with a small portion of the lung.
The patient will be under general anesthesia and will have a chest tube in place until the following day.
The patient will be admitted for one night and discharged the next day.
I will continue to follow up with the patient's rheumatologist and medication therapy for rheumatoid arthritis as previously outlined.
Follow-up: I will see the patient again in a few minutes to sign paperwork and begin the treatment process.
